data.title: La Rosa Holdings reports preliminary 2025 revenue of ~$79M, Up 14% YoY (LRHC)
data.description:
• La Rosa Holdings (LRHC) reported preliminary, unaudited fiscal year 2025 revenue of ~$79M.
  • The result represents an estimated 14% year-over-year increase compared with fiscal 2024 revenue.
  • LRHC shares down 9.8%.

data.title: Santander fined more than €40M over deficiencies in Openbank internal processes - report
data.description:
Banco Santander (SAN) (BCDRF) has been hit with a fine of more than €40M over deficiencies in internal processes at its Openbank unit, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg News.

SEPBLAC, Spain's supervisory body for combating money laundering and terrorist financing, has levied one of its biggest penalties on the Spanish lender, the January 23 report noted.

The fine amount is calculated based on the company size and not on the seriousness of the events, the people who asked not to be named discussing private information reportedly told the news outlet.

The regulatory review concerns interpretive matters regarding procedural and control rules, particularly regarding inactive customer accounts, and is not related to any money laundering case, a Santander spokesperson told Bloomberg.

data.title: Affirm latest fintech to seek U.S. bank charter in Trump era
data.description:
Affirm Holdings (AFRM) has submitted an application for a limited bank charter, it said on Friday, becoming the latest fintech to seek a banking license as firms ranging from payments firms to crypto platforms have pursued charters under the Trump administration.

The buy now, pay later company filed with the Nevada Financial Institutions Division and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corp. to set up a Nevada-chartered industrial loan company, which would operate as a bank subsidiary called Affirm Bank. Banking and fintech veteran John Marion has been tapped to serve as president of the new unit.

The company said the charter would help the rollout of additional financial-technology offerings for its U.S. customers, complementing Affirm's (AFRM) current business and bank partnership models.

“A banking subsidiary would strengthen and diversify Affirm’s platform, and help us bring honest financial products to more people,” said Affirm Founder and CEO Max Levchin in a statement. “This is about expanding what we can do for consumers and merchants, and building for the long term.”

A number of companies, including payments giant PayPal Holdings (PYPL), stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group (CRCL) and XRP (XRP-USD)-linked Ripple, have applied for bank charters since President Donald Trump returned to office a year ago, as federal regulators eased rules to encourage lending and innovation. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Materion reports prelim Q4 profit below estimate; shares down
data.description:
• Materion (MTRN) expects adjusted Q4 EPS to be $1.50–$1.55, compared with analyst estimate of $1.60.
  • MTRN expects full-year 2025 adjusted EPS $5.40–$5.45, compared with estimate of $5.51, reflecting strength in electronic materials and precision optics.
  • Preliminary, unaudited results are slightly below the midpoint of full-year guidance, with final results to be reported on February 12, 2026.
  • MTRN reported Q4 quality issue with a large precision clad strip customer that temporarily idled production, but says the issue is now fully resolved and shipments have resumed.
  • The company expects to record a non-recurring charge of ~$20M–$25M related to quality claims, scrap, and temporary plant idling, excluded from non-GAAP results.
  • Production assets are back online and ramping through Q1 2026, with no anticipated material impact to 2026 volume for the affected customer.
  • MTRN shares down 9.8%.

data.title: ‘Ignore the macro, buy the micro’ – Requisite Capital
data.description:
Investors should disregard macroeconomic headwinds and focus on individual company fundamentals, according to Bryn Talkington, founder and managing partner of Requisite Capital.

“You got to just buy the debt. You got to ignore the macro and buy the micro,” Talkington said in an interview with CNBC.

Her aggressive stance comes as markets have battled back from recent lows, with strong corporate earnings providing a foundation for continued gains.

Talkington pointed to the strength of company earnings as justification for her micro-focused approach, noting that results have come in “very strong” despite isolated disappointments like Intel (INTC), as its Q1 earnings outlook is below forecast.

At the same time, she acknowledged that the macro picture remains supportive, with GDP growth remaining robust. This alignment of micro and macro strength creates a compelling backdrop for investors.

The performance of small caps (IWM), (IJS) has been particularly notable, with the Russell 2000 (IWM) up about 8.5% in 2026 so far even as yields have risen both domestically and globally.

Talkington interpreted this resilience as a signal that “investors are thinking the real economy is going to continue to grow, and the stock market will continue to broaden out this year.”

The willingness of investors to embrace small caps despite rising rates suggests confidence in underlying economic conditions.

Rather than buying small caps directly, Talkington has positioned herself through the equal-weight S&P 500 Index (RSP), which she added to her portfolio nearly two years ago as a “broadening trade.”

She noted that while RSP is equal-weighted by stock, it is heavily concentrated in financials and industrials. This positioning has proven prescient as the anticipated broadening has begun to materialize.

Talkington characterized RSP as a “granny shot trade” with greater durability if markets experience turbulence later in the year. She expects volatility (VIX) to persist throughout 2026 given current market multiples, making the more defensive positioning of the equal-weight index an attractive hedge against potential choppiness ahead.

data.title: Morgan Stanley finds Oracle's AI infrastructure buildout creates risk for EPS targets
data.description:
Morgan Stanley analysts surmise that Oracle's (ORCL) rapid buildout of artificial intelligence infrastructure will likely push earnings per share results to fall below expected targets and increase capital expenditures.

"From the equity perspective, we struggle to see a viable path to Oracle's EPS targets, a view factored into the current share price, leaving a balanced risk/reward," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Keith Weiss, in a Friday investor report. "From the credit perspective, we think key risks, including our new higher forecasts for funding needs and leverage, are not reflected in spreads, even after underperformance."

Morgan Stanley reduced its price target on Oracle by 33% to $213 from $320 but retained its Equal-weight rating. The financial firm also created a new category for Oracle's AI infrastructure business, GPUaaS, or GPU as a Service.

"Our model suggests $275 billion of cumulative (FY26-FY28) cash capex in the base case vs. consensus at $189 billion," Weiss said. "We forecast gross adjusted debt including leases >$400 billion and gross adjusted leverage >5x at FY28E. This illustrates sizable funding needs across bonds, loans, and creative chip financing solutions, as well as intensifying ratings risk."

Morgan Stanley lowered its fiscal 2028 EPS to a base case of $8.51 versus Oracle's target of $10.65. The analysts modified their 2030 EPS to $10.02, which is less than half of Oracle's $21 target.

Still, Oracle has captured a monumental backlog of contracted customer commitments reaching $426B. The company exited its most recent quarter with remaining performance obligations of $523B.

"While this backlog gives Oracle a meaningful degree of visibility over the next five years, the vast majority of this acceleration in backlog is attributable to customers contracting with Oracle to secure infrastructure for AI compute, namely OpenAI (OPENAI) (comprising at least $300 billion / ~60% of this backlog)," Weiss said.

Oracle shares made a staggering run-up during the first nine months of 2025, climbing above $300 by mid-September. However, they have since suffered a precipitous fall. Shares were relatively static during Friday trading at about $178.

data.title: Two Harbors Investment cut to Neutral at UBS on UWMC deal
data.description:
UBS analyst Doug Harter downgraded Two Harbors Investment (TWO) shares to Neutral from Buy in a recent note after mortgage lender UWMC Holdings (UWMC) said it's buying the REIT in a ~$1.3B all-stock deal.

Until the deal closes—which is expected in the second quarter—"we believe that TWO shares will trade primarily on deal dynamics, rather than fundamentals," Haier wrote in a note. He added that he doesn't see the transaction encountering any big regulatory challenges.

Two Harbors (TWO) stock has jumped 21% since the merger was announced in mid-December, compared with the S&P 500's 2.9% gain.

UBS's Neutral rate agrees with the average Wall Street analyst rating of Hold but diverges from the average SA analyst rating of Buy.

TWO shares were little changed in Friday late morning trading.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: GE, MSFT, SMCI, AMGN
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) receives a rating upgrade from Dhierin Bechai, who argues that valuation concerns based on P/E ratios are misleading, while Amgen (AMGN) earns a Buy rating from Edmund Ingham on the strength of its MariTide obesity drug potential. On the downgrade side, Deep Value Investing moves Microsoft (MSFT) to Hold ahead of earnings amid cloud margin pressures, and Analysis Fundamental downgrades Super Micro Computer (SMCI) to Hold citing persistent shipment delays and low profit margins.

Upgrades

  • GE Aerospace (GE): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Dhierin Bechai. The analyst corrected his valuation methodology, shifting from a conglomerate-era multiple to one reflecting GE Aerospace’s current business structure, revealing an 11% upside with a $346.31 price target.

    “When we look at GE Aerospace’s valuation, we see that the stock is currently fairly valued with 2027 earnings in mind using the EV/EBITDA for the peer group. That makes sense, as I believe the market should be forward-looking.”

  • Amgen (AMGN): Upgrade Hold to Buy by Edmund Ingham. The analyst cites the transformative potential of Amgen’s obesity drug candidate MariTide alongside robust pipeline prospects and a healthy 2.9% dividend yield.

    “If we remove Mari-Tide from the equation entirely, Amgen appears to be a fairly valued company, its P/E and P/S ratios about standard for the sector, its patent expiry woes offset by the strong performance of recently approved assets and pipeline prospects. … If we add the ‘MariTide factor,’ there is an opportunity for a major valuation / share price upward correction if data are positive and support approval.”

Downgrades

  • Microsoft (MSFT): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Deep Value Investing. The analyst expresses concern about declining cloud gross margins and believes increased CapEx guidance is no longer viewed positively by the market ahead of FQ2 2026 earnings.

    “With Cloud gross margins pressured (68% last quarter, guided to 66% in FQ2) and CapEx still ramping up, I see the risk of a cautionary margin guide for FQ3. I no longer believe that a hike in CapEx guidance is positive for the hyperscalers. … Unless management puts out a spectacular FQ3 growth guidance for Azure, back to 40% YOY, I don’t see shares recovering from the recent selloff.”

  • Super Micro Computer, Inc. (SMCI): Downgrade Buy to Hold by Analysis Fundamental. The analyst highlights persistent shipment delays, a volatile operational model, and weak pricing power as key concerns despite strong AI infrastructure demand.

    “Growth catalysts of advanced cooling systems, partnership with NVDA, reduced time-to-market via DCBBS solutions and global expansion, still can’t overcome the company’s low margins and volatile operational model. … I downgrade SMCI stock to Hold, mainly due to the persistence of shipment delays and generally its volatile operational model. Additionally, I would like to see higher margins before initiating a position.”

data.title: FormFactor falls amid new short call at Hedgeye
data.description:
FormFactor (FORM) dropped 2.1% at least partly amid a new short idea from Hedgeye, which sees the potential for 45% downside. 

"FORM shares have more than tripled from cycle lows and now trade at ~50x blended 2026/2027 earnings—an extreme valuation that leaves no margin for error," Hedgeye analyst Felix Wang wrote in a note on Friday. "In our view, virtually all good news is priced in."

After Intel's "sharp guide-down" on Thursday, Hedgeye is reassessing hedges in the "overheated" semiconductor capital equipment space. FormFactor (FORM) screens as a compelling short, Wang wrote.

"Buyside expectations are skewed toward a significant beat-and-raise on February 4th, driven by assumed HBM/DRAM and advanced packaging strength (TSMC)," Hedgeye's Wang added. "Intel’s commentary meaningfully undermines that setup. A modest or nonexistent guidance raise would likely disappoint consensus."

"With stretched valuation, fading HBM tailwinds, ongoing share losses, and elevated expectations into earnings, FORM represents an asymmetric short," Wang wrote. "We estimate ~45% downside to fair value."
